data.title: Sterling Infrastructure: Impressive Yet Expensive
data.description:
Shares of Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) have doubled since I last checked on the shares in the early summer of last year. Strong operating momentum and a positive reaction to the accretive CEC Facilities Group deal supported advancement in the shares here.

Supported by stronger growth, margin gains, and a convincing outlook, shares have rightfully seen continued gains, although that valuation multiple inflation is clearly observable here, with shares having doubled over the past ten months.

While appreciating the performance of the business a great deal, I do not like the serious valuation multiples inflation seen here, making me cautious here.

Other interesting serial value creators and M&A stores are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Very Strong Performer

By the end of February, Sterling reported its results for 2025. The company reported an 18% increase in full-year sales to $2.49 billion, with growth reported at 32% if we account for the RHB divestment in 2024. Supported by the CEC deal, which was completed early in September of last year, the company reported a 51% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $755 million, with revenues already trending north of $3 billion per year.

The revenue mix is changing rapidly to the e-infrastructure solutions business, with reported segment sales up 59% to $1.47 billion, making up 60% of total sales. The remainder of sales are generated by transportation solutions and building solutions, both seeing declines in annual sales in 2025.

Company-wide operating margins rose by more than 4 points to 16% and change thanks to the change towards higher e-infrastructure revenues. On the bottom line, the company reported GAAP net earnings of $290 million, equal to $9.38 per share based on a diluted share count. Adjusted earnings came in a dollar and fifty cents per share higher at $10.88. Part of the reconciliation is the result of a $24 million pre-tax stock-based compensation, which is equal to about sixty cents per share, with realistic earnings seen north of $10 per share.

The company ended the year with a net cash position of around $100 million, as a diluted share count of 31 million shares trading around the $450 mark works down to a $14 billion equity valuation, this including a modest net cash position. This values the business at around 5-6 times sales, around 28 times adjusted EBITDA just north of half a billion, and around 45 times earnings.

The company guided for 2026 sales to come in between $3.05 and $3.20 billion. While this is up dramatically from the reported 2025 results, it compares to an already $3.02 billion run rate as of the fourth quarter. Full-year adjusted earnings are seen moving up to a midpoint of $13.75 per share, plus or minus thirty cents.

Stripping out the stock-based compensation expense, realistic earnings are likely seen around $13 per share, marking the full-year contribution from dealmaking and some additional (margin) growth. That still translates into a demanding multiple in the mid-thirties for a net unleveraged business here.

Expectations Rise

Since June, shares have more than doubled as a $217 stock has risen to the $450 mark here, marking large returns in a relatively short period of time of much less than a year. This has not just been a straight line, as a $400 stock in the fall fell to the $300 mark at the start of this year. Following strong momentum ever since, shares now trade about thirty dollars from the highs set around the $480 mark. The timing of some capital allocation has been sound, with the company having spent $26 million on buybacks in the fourth quarter at average prices around the $310 mark.

The real achievements and anticipation are not just about the current result but, moreover, the transformation that the company is undergoing. Non-existent in 2018, e-infrastructure solutions have grown to 60% of sales, these carry outsized margins as well, representing 73% of total segment margins.

Moreover, momentum is seen continuing with the total backlog of $3.0 billion now being equal to about a year's worth of revenues, this number up 78% on a reported basis, and up 49% if we exclude the contribution from the CEC acquisition. The backlog is even set to increase to $4.5 billion if one factors in future phase opportunities that are project extensions from current awards, seen well over a billion.

What Now?

Having forecasted earnings around $9 per share in June of last year, with a runway seen around $10 per share, valuation multiples stood at 22 times earnings, with some net leverage seen. Of course, earnings power going forward has improved a great deal to $13 per share. While this is to be applauded, earnings multiples have expanded a great deal to a multiple in the mid-thirties, with the business now operating with a net unleveraged business.

Having been arguably too conservative last summer, I see no need to chase the shares here, after they doubled in about ten months. This is driven by strong operating performance and the strength of the balance sheet, but a big part of this has been the result of valuation multiple expansion as well, of course. This makes me upbeat on the business, but shares have definitely seen quite a large re-rating.

On the one hand, continued growth makes for continued earnings growth, yet paying up 35 times earnings for a business enjoying solid demand in both sales and margins already creates real risks if momentum fades.

However, the AI market seems to be in full demand here, being strong, as I am very cautious here. Given all this, I like what the business has done, kicking myself for being too conservative in the past, but I fail to make another potential mistake by aggressively getting involved here, or at equivalent multiples.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Oil Retreats, Manufacturing Advances, Consumers Stay The Course
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, and the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

March data included a downturn in industrial and manufacturing production, as well as a downturn in existing home sales.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.03%, up +0.01% w/w (1-yr range: 5.64-6.43).
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.24%, down -0.08 w/w (3.95-4.65).
  • Credit spread 1.79%, up +0.09% w/w (1.59-2.02).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.54%, up +0.03% w/w (-1.07 - +0.92).
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.69%, up +0.05% w/w (-1.65 - 0.80).
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: +0.06%, down -0.11% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily).

  • 6.29% down -0.10% w/w (5.99%-7.08%).

The yield curve has completely regularized. Two weeks ago mortgage rates moved into the middle of their range, turning them from a positive to neutral, but last week they declined enough to turn back positive.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -1% to 160 (147-194) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -3 to 161 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% down -3% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. down -1% [NSA]
  • Refi apps up +5% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +15% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Unchanged w/w
  • Up +2.0% YoY.

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications had been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Purchase mortgage applications were positive only six weeks ago, but have declined since then all the way to negative last week, as did refinance applications. The former remained negative this week, the latter reverted to neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen. Four months ago they made yet another multi-year low, but improved afterward, suggesting the bottom is in - and they made a new one year high several weeks ago YoY, and yet another last week. For that reason, their reading improved to neutral and now positive.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. February data was just released three weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +1.0%, YoY Real M1 up +2.4% (-1.4% 8/23 - +1.8% 7/25).
  • M2 m/m up +0.9%, YoY Real M2 up +2.4% (2.1% June 2025 3-year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when the real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it has either been during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long-leading metrics now.

Corporate profits Q4 actual + Q1 estimated from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p.

  • Q4 up +0.18 to 74.39, up +4.8% q/q.
  • Q1 estimated down -0.19 to 71.10, down -4.4%.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 and Q2, earnings increased sharply, and aside from a few brief weeks, the rating was positive. Profits for Q4 started out negative but improved enough to change the rating to positive. Q1’s are expected to decline sufficiently to change the rating back to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -0.03 (looser) -0.46 (-0.39 - -0.60).
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.04 (looser) to -0.47 (-0.38 - -0.60).
  • Leverage subindex up +0.01 (tight) to +0.01 (+0.02 - -0.54).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive one poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral last year, all three had been solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral. The leverage index remains in the neutral range, which extends from -.10 to +.10.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down 15 to 268, down -54 m/m (268 4/10/26 - 448 12/26/25) (new 12-month low) Components: Yield curve 125, Phillips curve 143.
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.4065 to -0.6475 (+0.1828 4/3/26 - -.8976 5/16/25).
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: down -6.2 w/w to 29.7 as of 4/2/26. iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is the max value, and below 25 is a recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead to see whether a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession-eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal), which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components earlier this year. Gas prices and yield curve gyrations remain the most volatile inputs. A few weeks ago I was posting about levels that would finally change this indicator to neutral or even positive. It seems important to note that its post-Covid low was 125 on July 1, 2022.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy and, during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered. At the end of last year it was on the very cusp of turning neutral, but has since risen.

For almost 24 months, IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession. Last summer it did edge closer to their threshold, but then rebounded. Updates resumed in February and finally updated again this week. They are very close to forecasting a recession in the next 5 months.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Down -0.0446 to 118.86 w/w, down -4.1% YoY (last week) (broad) (119.61 - 130.21) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index.
  • Down -0.49 to 98.18 w/w, down -1.2% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (95.55- 109.88).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged and 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, but since the war with Iran started, it has improved, perhaps reflecting a “flight to safety.”

I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US$ as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production. But in the past 12 months it could be more a sign of relative U.S. weakness.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Down -0.66 to 131.48 w/w (97.32 - 134.94).
  • Up +27.8% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • Up +5.80 to 179.47 w/w (130.47-175.28).
  • Up +27.5% YoY.

Last spring the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range and now positive. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Importantly, if commodities were not priced in US$ terms but in other major currencies, it would have been quite a completely different story since last year. Also note that BCOM includes precious metals (gold and silver) and, very importantly for our present moment, oil.

Again, mechanically these are scored as positives, because they typically reflect firming demand. But for 2026, as in 2025, they may mainly reflect US$ and U.S. trade weakness. Let me put it this way: a 20% increase in industrial commodities indicates either a white-hot global economy (a demand-driven increase), or a relative loss of faith in one or more fiat currencies (a currency supply-driven increase), or a choke on (oil) supply.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +4.5% to 7126.06.

Since July, stocks have made repeated new all-time highs, including seven weeks ago. Last week, following the closure of the Straits of Hormuz, they made new three month and 2026 lows. As a result, this indicator turned neutral. Recently it sank to 3- and 6-month lows, warranting a change to neutral. This week it made a new all time high, which confirms the neutral reading. If it does not make a new 3-month low in the next several months, that will warrant a change to positive. Note that my ratings are designed to avoid being whipsawed by brief “V” shaped selloffs and rebounds.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • *Empire State up +12.9 to 19.3
  • *Philly up +24.4 to 33.0
  • Richmond up +13 to 4
  • Kansas City up +8 to 15
  • Dallas down -5.0 to 6.1
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +7 to 15.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since mid-year, these improved to neutral and then to positive, and the overall tenor surprisingly has been an improvement to positive. The December reports suggested more, if mild, contraction. January improved just enough to cross the threshold to positive, and that has continued into this month.

Since last May I have added the regional service indexes. Those reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • *Empire State up +8.6 to -14.0
  • Philly down -6.6 to -23.9
  • Richmond up +12 to 2
  • Kansas City up +12 to 15
  • *Dallas down -10.0 to -13.3
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +2 to -7.

The most positive recent trend in all of the high-frequency data in August was the sharp rebound in manufacturing all the way to solidly positive. Meanwhile, the regional non-manufacturing reports, which had stabilized at negative levels since May, and then turned positive, have since turned sharply negative. They faded further last autumn before improving in January. In February they faded again, but since then they have rebounded sharply.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 207,000, down -11,000 w/w, down -4.6% YoY.
  • 4-week average 209,750, up +500 w/w, down -5.1% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. Then they turned moderately negative through midyear this year. But since the beginning of last July, more often than not readings have been lower YoY, generally turning this metric positive. I continue to suggest some caution because they may be affected by the disappearance of some immigrant employees.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Unchanged at 87 w/w.
  • Up +5.1% YoY (low -13.6%- high 4/3/26 +5.1%).

This series had been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. I suspect this was due to a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. The YoY reading was trending “less bad” for many months, then neutral, and finally, in late last summer, it turned positive. The big downturn at the end of last year was seasonal; and has it has rebounded since.

Tax Withholding

  • Last 20 reporting days, 2026 vs. 2025: $274.9 B vs. $268.3 B, up +$6.6 B or +2.5%.

These were very positive for most of 2025. Importantly, since the beginning of October, the YoY comparisons weakened considerably, and recently were among the weakest all year. It is possible that this was tax-related deferrals until 2026, given the tax changes enacted by Congress this past summer. But with YoY average wage gains of just under 4%, this measure had been just barely keeping its proverbial head above water. Five weeks ago, it went underwater, but then recovered to a positive. The 20 day measure retreated to neutral two weeks ago, then recovered last week, before retreating again this week.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$11.69 to $84.56 w/w, up +31.0% YoY ($54.98 - $11.54).
  • Gas prices unchanged at $4.12 w/w, up +$.95 YoY.
  • Usage 4-week average up +3.6% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and declined even further in 2025. Late last month they made a new 3 year low, which was very positive for consumers. Last week they rose to yet another new three-year high, which, needless to say, is negative.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, but that totally reversed with the war on Iran. This week gas prices made yet another new three year high, and so are negative.

In 2024, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive early in 2025, but recently turned back down. Last week it turned positive and stayed so this week.

Note: Given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 3.67% Secured Overnight Financing Rate ((SOFR)), up +0.09% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record as to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I have included it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments

  • March 2026 vs. 2025: $25.1 B vs. $9.6 B (+$15.5 B).
  • April 1-15, 2026 vs. 2025: $3.2 B vs. $2.3 B (+$0.9 B).

I inaugurated this measure last spring after “Liberation Day.” The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items.

As per my above comments, the regional Fed reports indicate that these tariffs have had a substantial upward impact on prices paid for materials, with only about half of those increases - but nevertheless half - being passed on to consumers.

Note that the U.S. Supreme Court ruling adverse to the Administration did not specify what the remedy would be, so it is very likely that that aspect of the litigation will drag out for a year - or possibly more.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.10 to 2.80 w/w (Low 1.78 May 31, 2025 - high 3.08 April 5, 2025).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00), although twice in the past four months it was at the exact lower boundary of that rating. Since then, it has improved sharply.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry

  • Last week's 7-day average was up +7% YoY, vs. +31% one week prior.

Last year the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In particular, since Thanksgiving, it turned sharply higher and has remained so except for several weeks. It remains my opinion that, should the consumer economy turn down, dining out would be one of the easiest - and first - things to cut.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +7.0% YoY, 4-week average +7.0% (high 7.6% 4/10/26, 1/2/26, & 12/5/25; 4.8% 7/3/2025) United States Redbook Index.

The overall trend in this metric since three years ago has been not only positive, but increasingly so over time. Last August and September it increased sharply to very positive before fading somewhat in October and November. It picked up sharply after mid-November, and is still very much in the upper half of its 52 week range.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.44% to +1.87% YoY (High 2.68% 7/2/25 - Low 0.72% on 2/13/26).

This is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say, it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high seven months ago. Since the beginning of this year, it plummeted to new 52-week lows, before increasing in the past two months.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +5.1% YoY (high - 6.3% 2/20/25; 12-month low—1.7% 12/16/24).

One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads to employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. made another 12-month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before backing off. considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months. With the big jump in consumer spending YoY in the past several months, this measure has improved as well.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up 5.1% YoY (2026 average +4.0%).
  • Intermodal units down -1.1% YoY (2026 average -0.3%).
  • Total loads up +1.7% YoY (2026 average +1.6%).

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +18 to 2257 (low 2055 - high 2257) (new 12 month high).
  • Baltic Dry Index up +362 to 2523 (low 715 - high 2815).

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive last March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kicked in. Over the course of the remainder of 2025, there was a gradual weakening from that peak. Rail has generally turned somewhat higher this year so far, but this week once again it was mixed.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus is very positive.

The BDI rebounded all the way to the top of its six-month range earlier in 2025, before retreating back into the middle of that range. A few months ago it again made several 52 week highs before backing off again.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand. Note that both of these metrics are likely very much affected by renewed belligerence in the Middle East.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.7% w/w.
  • Up +9.4% YoY.

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Last spring it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again last May. In autumn it returned to positive, and with few exceptions has remained so since.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10-year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr. - 3 mo. Treasury ✓
2 yr - Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓ X
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 8 5 1
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad* ✓
US$ Major currencies* ✓
Total commodities* ✓ *
Industrial commodities* ✓ *
Stock prices ✓ X
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 10 (*8) 1 3(*5)
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding x ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 7 3 0

All time frames remained positive this week.

The most positive aspect of the long leading indicators is the spreads in the bond market. On the other hand, the increase in interest rates finally caused a sharp change in ratings for mortgage applications last week. It will be interesting to see how much corporate profits rebound during this earnings season.

The short leading indicators also remained stable, buoyed by the continuing fact that (relatively speaking) almost nobody is getting laid off. Additionally, manufacturing-related indicators like the regional Fed indexes have consistently indicated a rebound over the past several months. Skyrocketing commodity prices are almost certainly about supply shortages, not a surge in demand, so I recommend taking those as big negatives.

The coincident indicators continue to show strong consumer spending, while rail and tax withholding continue to be mixed or tepid. So far, despite the big increase in gas prices, there is no evidence of consumers “pulling in their horns.”

Interestingly, many of the very positive indicators continue to be financial (e.g., the yield curve, and stock prices). “Real” economy indicators are much more mixed - but see low initial jobless claims. But unless and until consumers pull back, it is difficult to see the economy actually contracting.

data.title: Here Group: Why Xiao Ao Could Outperform Other IP Releases
data.description:
Have you ever stopped to wonder why Here Group (HERE) completed several trading sessions higher?

Right off the bat, it's clear that it wasn't related to the Q2 FY 2026 report I covered here.

I told you that the stock might be in the early stages of incubating IPs like Wakuku and transforming them into some kind of Labubu-like venture with Enlight.

Patience could turn Here Group into a kind of Pop Mart (PMRTY) or even a Chinese Sanrio (SNROF).

But that still doesn't answer the question.

If you're one of the few folks digging into this story, you probably already know that Here is launching its new IP called Xiao Ao tomorrow (April 17th).

And this ties in perfectly with the IP flywheel thesis I've been talking about for a while now.

But I think there are a few things around Xiao Ao that the Western market is overlooking, and if they’re priced right, there’s some alpha there.

That's what I want to get into here.

But first, just a quick look at the performance of my last article here.

While the S&P has delivered just over 8% since the end of March, Here Group stock has outperformed it, up 18% in the same stretch.

Strong, don't you think?

As I said last time, once those pop-up store costs start getting spread out, the bottom line should look better. Over time, the market might start to see them as a more asset-light pop-toy name, maybe even a licensor down the road.

It’s a strong thesis, and with Enlight likely pushing some content (whether through film or Douyin), it really comes down to execution.

And I'm not even looking for a Ne Zha here.

A handful of small to mid-sized productions could already move the needle quite a bit.

Why Xiao Ao Might Sell More Than SIINONO

Like many products out there, Here Group's pop toys rely on influencers. And the partnership with Yuehua Entertainment a few quarters ago opened some doors in that regard.

For SIINONO, Here Group chose an actor named Liu Xuancheng.

He’s still very young (born in 2004), but already pretty well known, especially from Douyin clips like ‘Rebellious Love’.

SIINONO did RMB 12.8 million in Q1 FY 2026 (its launch quarter), then grew about 50% to RMB 19.2 million in Q2.

That's strong, no doubt. But it’s still not enough to fully de-risk a possible drop in Wakuku’s sales.

Here's an overview of sales by IP:

Quick math here, at around RMB 80 per unit, SIINONO probably moved ~400,000 units in H1 FY 2026. If that ~50% growth trend holds, they could exit the year above RMB 100 million, or about ~1.3 million units sold.

Converting, that would be something over $15 million and just over 13% of the $108.7 million revenue that Here Group guided in the last quarter.

Quite possible, if you ask me.

But I still haven't told you why I think Xiao Ao will outsell SIINONO.

It's even simpler than it seems. Xiao Ao's ambassador is Wang Yibo, one of the biggest Chinese actors currently and quite well-known among Gen Z in Asia.

Here's a peek at his Weibo account:

Xuancheng has ~2 million followers; Yibo has at least 20x more reach than the SIINONO ambassador!

And this is where it gets tricky.

I can't simply assume that because of the ambassador's reach, Xiao Ao will sell 20x more.

With an ~80% haircut, that 20x turns into something more like ~3–4x SIINONO. That gets you to about 3 to 4 million units, or roughly RMB 240 to 320 million ($35–47 million) in revenue.

All in, Xiao Ao and SIINONO together would account for around $56 million yearly.

Priced In?

So, is Xiao Ao already included in the FY 2026 guidance? I think that's the question everyone should be asking themselves now.

Let's look at the facts.

Management announced in Q1 FY 2026 (December 2025) that revenue would be between RMB 750 and 800 million (same range of $109.8 to $117.2 million converted that I gave you before) for the entire year.

At the end of Q2 FY 2026 (around March), they reiterated this.

Xiao Ao's first announcement came in mid-April.

As sales begin tomorrow (mid-Q3 FY 2026), the bulk of the impact will fall in Q4 and likely increase into FY 2027. Also, companies like Here Group can only base their guidance on what's known at the time of disclosure.

Xiao Ao was only announced in April, almost a month after the earnings call.

If they have good pre-sales and the pop toy continues to sell well in April/May, they should adjust this guidance upwards.

But it seems to me that Wall St. has already moved ahead and is pricing some of that into FY 2027.

For example, for FY 2027, they’re targeting around $240 million (RMB 1.63 billion), which implies roughly 103% to 117% growth compared to the FY 2026 guidance.

It might seem like they're going crazy, I know.

But consider that if Wakuku normalizes it to ~25% quarter-over-quarter and SIINONO normalizes it to something like 30% also quarter-over-quarter for the whole of FY 2026, Here Group could reach something close to that in FY 2027.

I'm not even baking Xiao Ao here.

And here's the catch.

Wall St. has a 'Strong Buy' rating on Here Group, with a price target of an incredible $7.80 per share.

An upside of ~120% from where the stock is trading today.

They seem to be pricing in a couple of decent IP launches and strong organic growth from Wakuku, but little to no contribution from the Enlight JV (where the optionality in the thesis comes from).

Plugging in a multiple of 1 to 1.5x into my model with projected FY 2027 revenue of $240 million and keeping the capital structure the same, I arrive at ~$7.90 per share.

Seems to be what Wall St. is aiming for.

But I need to bring that to present value, and here are some difficulties.

Given Here Group’s volatility as a Chinese small-cap, I’m using a beta of 1.5 for the cost of equity. That gets me to a discount rate of around 13%, and a price target closer to $6.20.

That's a step up from my previous base case of ~$4.80 in the last article. I'm leaning a bit less conservative this time because of Xiao Ao.

But if I use the downside of the multiple (~1x sales) and discount by the same rate, I arrive at a price target of $4.90.

So, I think everything is calibrated here.

I'll reiterate my 'Buy' rating for Here Group with potential upside of up to three digits in the coming years.

The balance sheet is clean, and they're sitting on plenty of cash (as I showed you in the last article) to withstand the pressure of a mix more focused on pop-up stores.

My only disagreement with Wall St. is regarding the 'Strong Buy' rating.

Sure, partnerships with Chinese celebrities can keep driving buzz, and the JV with Enlight could eventually turn them into a licensor. But the 'Wakuku risk,' being a fad while accounting for ~70% of revenue, is hard to ignore.

That's why I still see this as a smaller position within a portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buying The Fear Before It Shows Up: The PBDC Setup
data.description:
The current BDC environment is potentially being seen as the start of a broader credit unwind phase, leading to some investor apathy and sharp corrections in share prices across the board. Redemptions have picked up across a few private credit vehicles, signaling investor caution. However, both history and current data show that this is more likely an early repricing regime rather than a breakdown.

This is a market that is pricing in credit risks ahead of time, and it appears that a lot of downside is already factored in, while carry returns remain intact. I think it is a good time to start accumulating selectively for a long-term portfolio. A broader portfolio like the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC) should be able to diversify idiosyncratic credit risks. And as my analysis will show, PBDC is relatively skewed toward larger, sponsor-backed platforms with more resilient underwriting; the high-beta segment exposure, where stress is likely to emerge first, is relatively low.

The risks ahead are not immaterial; the question is how well the portfolio is already pricing in those risks. The PBDC portfolio still shows a few names trading at a significant premium, but some of the core holdings, like ARCC and OBDC, are trading at a deep discount. Overall, as I had mentioned in a recent article comparing ARCC versus OBDC, the BDC trade is not all about yield maximization anymore. It is about allocating based on credit quality that is priced in.

the outlook at the moment is not to position BDC trades assuming a clean environment - investors should be thinking about credit risks as rates could stay far higher than initially expected, stressing borrowers and making refinancing harder.

Where Are We in the Cycle

The extent of early pricing in of credit risk is evident from the 1-year rolling return charts based on BIZD share prices (as a proxy for BDC returns broadly, given its longer history to fall back on). The recent rebound post some easing of the Middle East tensions has meant 1-year rolling returns are now at ~-5% (they were ~20% at the peak of the crisis that was impacting broader markets, and the chart reflects that, ending March 31, 2026). The only true drawdown bigger than this, over the past 12 years, was in 2020, when rolling returns collapsed to ~-25-40%. That was more about a liquidity shock, not just credit deterioration. Even at current rolling return levels of ~-5%, this is still one of the lowest points seen across the 12-year period - and it is not like credit risks have not surfaced in this period. The energy stress in 2015-16, tightening impact in 2018, and even some parts of 2022 did not lead to sustained capital impairment at the index level. Repricing has mostly been gradual, with income more than offsetting drawdowns.

I do not rule out 2026 being a year unseen in credit risk terms (in the past 12 years) - but that sounds extreme and is not yet showing up in non-accruals across major BDCs. In fact, the market is quite early to reprice BDCs already this time - further credit deterioration is more likely to be absorbed gradually rather than being expressed through a sharp drawdown. And therefore, waiting for the cycle lows may not work out (like in past cycles) the way the current credit risk outlook suggests - I think investors will do well to start accumulating and buying on further dips to come out with a very strong portfolio on the other side of the crisis, if any. And a diversified vehicle like PBDC could balance that exposure with core exposure in quality cycle-tested platforms while keeping some alpha possibilities by including lower weights in dislocated and heavily discounted names where credit risks are overpriced in.

The Macro Does not Signal Distress

The macro picture does not support a 12-year outlier in stress terms, something the rolling return already indicates. The SOFR has eased to ~3.7% from 5% in 2024. That shows that the earnings tailwinds have diminished and borrower financing costs are elevated. However, high-yield markets, as reflected by HYG below, show a gradual drift rather than a breakdown, indicating controlled repricing of credit risk. There is no evidence yet of a breakdown below prior stress highs. Leveraged loan markets (proxied by BKLN) remain stable, which signals that underlying asset values are holding despite rising stress. Overall, this is not a clean credit environment, and income tailwinds are diminishing, but we are not in distressed territory yet. The question is how much deterioration (versus that indicated) is already priced in.

Valuation Already Reflects Selective Stress

Valuations across a few key holdings are already reflecting risks. ARCC is currently trading at ~0.94x NAV, while OBDC is trading at an even cheaper discount at ~0.75-0.8x to NAV. Even GBDC is trading at a discount. At the other end of the spectrum, HTGC and MAIN continue to trade at a premium because of their stronger underwriting track records and capital allocation discipline. Even here, the premiums have compressed from the peak already and meaningfully. HTGC has corrected further from the data shown in Q4 below and is now trading at NAV multiples last seen in 2022, and far below the 2024 cycle peak of ~1.72x. MAIN is holding relatively well - the P/NAV chart I constructed over the past 5 quarters shows a dip in Q1 2025, but that coincided with a market wide correction due to tariff uncertainties (not purely credit repricing effects). Removing the Q1 2025 aberration, the current valuations have contracted from ~1.9x - levels seen in 2025, even in Q3.

What Drives a Rebound

Because valuations have corrected ahead of actual credit stress, stabilization in the macro environment or an actual realized credit loss environment that is far lower than that already assumed is enough for the discount gap (or multiple compression) to close in several cases. Even if multiples do not revert to prior highs (possible, because we are clearly not in peak conditions for BDCs), we could see the carry trade supporting total returns.

As of today, most of the top holdings in PBDC do not indicate reasons to account for the breakdown already being priced in. ARCC continues to report strong interest coverage (~2.2x), declining leverage, and stable non-accruals (negligible deterioration YoY) - supporting a better than anticipated borrower resilience environment. OBDC shows non-accruals at ~1.1% of fair value (not an aberration from historical trends and in fact marginally trending down). Asset sales have been reported close to marks, dispelling concerns around NAV marks.

HTCG's portfolio looks even more solid - with only ~0.1% non-accruals and all loans performing, despite its exposure in a more volatile tech and venture ecosystem. MAIN also continues to generate NAV growth, realized gains, and strong dividend coverage - indicating no underlying credit stress. OTF reports low non-accruals and strong underwriting cushions (low LTV structures).

For most of the top holdings, valuations are factoring in another story, though. Even under a simplified framework, if non-accruals were to rise to ~10% of the portfolio with ~40-50% loss severity, say, that would translate to only ~4-5% NAV impact - something already priced in for ARCC and Golub, for example (and OBDC is already factoring in much more extreme conditions). The premium names have also corrected and show the non-accruals are even lower than the discounted holdings. But in case the credit stress worsens and reaches their portfolio too, PDBC's structure should see some rotation into discounted names, where allocation is higher (a self-reinforcing setup).

Overall, I expect the macro situation to be supportive - we are seeing some easing in energy prices as the Strait of Hormuz opens up (although we may not have heard the last of that). Hopes of rate cuts and easing borrower load are opening up again, and stagflation risks are easing a bit. Financing costs are still high, but we have seen that high yield and leveraged loan markets are repricing gradually, not indicating a breakdown.

Portfolio Positioning - Quality Skewed

Despite the diversification expectations from a BDC portfolio, PBDC is concentrated at the top - with ~70% of the portfolio in the top 10 and over 50% in large sponsor-backed platforms. These are scaled players with diversified borrower pools and stable non-accrual trends historically. I have already checked valuations for ARCC, OBDC, and GBDC (more than 25% of the portfolio and half of the segment in discussion) and found this segment trading at a discount too, in anticipation of credit stress ahead of time.

The second material layer in premium BDC territory consists of internally managed platforms (like MAIN and HTGC). This layer is known for stricter underwriting and capital discipline history, and valuations have contracted reasonably from peak cycle highs. While the quality here is great, a lower weight here does not mean a suboptimal allocation in my view. This is because, the premium risks are higher here. The discounted larger players segment has a better valuation floor to rely on, and unless we see calamitous credit outcomes, the risk-reward here could actually be better than the premium segment - especially when diversified.

PDBC also allocates ~15% to higher beta venture or niche lender plays where I believe stress will hit first. Another ~6% of the portfolio is opportunistic, invested in turnaround stories - BDCs that are already stressed or heavily discounted. The position size in these two buckets is contained, diversified, and keeps upside potential intact (without overburdening the portfolio with higher risks due to significant weights).

Overall, the portfolio is deeply anchored in names that can absorb stress gradually, while only a part of the portfolio (20%) is positioned for rebound. The higher beta and stressed part of the portfolio are already trading at deep discounts - so the risk-reward is balanced at least. Looking at the condition of the loan markets as of today and the relative valuation compression in the past few months, this could be the start of an accumulation phase for BDCs for investors wanting to build long-term income portfolios. Ironically, the peak of healthy conditions in 2023-25 was not the best time to Buy PBDC (looking at valuation compression since and total returns as well). Now is close to a good time to accumulate at least (a phase where risks are being aggressively priced in but not very visible yet).

data.title: CLOs: Pressure-Tested In Q1, Built For What Comes Next
data.description:
CLOs proved their mettle in Q1 2026, outperforming most credit sectors as geopolitical shocks and rising rates rattled markets, and the best opportunities may still lie ahead.

CLOs demonstrated relative resilience in Q1 2026, outperforming most credit sectors despite a challenging macro backdrop marked by rising Treasury yields, geopolitical escalation in the Middle East and growing recession concerns in the back half of the quarter. IG-rated tranches posted positive total returns, while mezzanine tranches faced pressure. A sharp selloff in software loans, the largest sector in the leveraged loan index at roughly 10-15%, was a defining feature of the quarter, driving meaningful dispersion across CLO portfolios and reinforcing the importance of manager and security selection. During the quarter, VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI) (30-day SEC yield: 5.12% as of 3/31/2026) underperformed its benchmark, J.P. Morgan CLO IG Index, by 17bps (0.75% vs. 0.92%). Meanwhile, VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) (30-day SEC yield: 6.20% as of 3/31/2026) was relatively in line with its benchmark, J.P. Morgan CLOIE Balanced Mezzanine Index (-0.33% vs -0.32%).

CLOs continued to compare favorably to investment grade corporates, high yield bonds, and leveraged loans, reinforcing their role as a compelling source of income and relative value amid heightened uncertainty. Our preference for higher-rated tranches remains given tight valuations and increasing tail risks, although select shorter spread-duration opportunities lower in the capital stack are beginning to present more attractive entry points.

Market Update

Total returns were marginally positive for CLOs at the index level in March. The asset class endured its worst monthly return in the past year. However, CLOs held in better than most other asset classes. Investors navigated a confluence of headwinds including an escalating and unresolved conflict in the Middle East centered on the Strait of Hormuz, sharply rising Treasury rates, and ongoing fears of AI disintermediation. The macro backdrop was difficult across risk assets broadly. Brent crude approached $120/bbl before pulling back toward $100/bbl by month-end, the S&P 500 fell 5.0% in March, and 10-year Treasury yields remain more than 30bp above pre-conflict levels, reflecting market uncertainty about the inflationary and growth implications of a prolonged Hormuz closure. The conflict has extended into April, though broader risk markets have caught a bid on hopes of a ceasefire. Despite the difficult tone, two positives are worth noting. Fourth quarter 2025 earnings season was constructive as issuers largely beat expectations and offered cautiously positive guidance and primary market activity held up well. Functioning capital markets suggest the technical picture, while challenged, is not yet in distress territory. Two-, 5- and 10-year Treasury rates traded 42bp, 44bp and 38bp higher, respectively.

Higher rated CLOs outperformed bank loans and investment grade credit and high yield bonds during the quarter.

CLO new issue supply decreased month-over-month, totaling $17.5bn in March, compared to $20.2bn in February. Refinancing and reset activity also decreased month-over-month, with $11.1bn pricing, after $23.7bn in February. Quarterly new issue volume of over $37bn was roughly in line with Q1 2025, although slightly behind the overall pace from last year, while refi reset volumes were 40% lower than Q1 last year. Retail demand continued, albeit at a slower pace, with CLO ETFs reporting $600mn in inflows in March, following inflows of $1.5bn in February. Total CLO ETF AUM is now over $44bn.

Loan market technicals remained supportive even as demand growth slowed, because repayments outpaced net new loan supply for the month. Institutional loan issuance remained constrained as the limited number of loans trading above par brought repricing activity to a halt. Wider market spreads along with the uncertain macroeconomic outlook limited issuance activity overall. However, the primary market was not fully frozen. Another wave of large M&A and LBO transactions launched during the month, bringing total quarterly volume for these types of transactions to a multi-year high. Retail loan funds saw $3.3bn in outflows in March, down from $4.1bn in February, albeit still elevated.

The trailing twelve-month default rate within the Morningstar US Leveraged Loan Index increased 6bp to 1.44%. As measured by JP Morgan, the default rate including distressed exchanges, decreased 19bp to 3.04%. Activity has been elevated as borrowers with unsustainable capital structures endeavored to manage their liabilities and avoid the bankruptcy process through liability management exercises, keeping the “official” default rate lower than otherwise. The default rate has declined 148bp off January 2025’s high.

CLO credit fundamentals largely remain strong but were marginally softer month-over-month. The overall picture appears stable as evidenced by the trailing 3-month downgrade / upgrade ratio for loans in CLOs at 1.72, down from 2.57 last month. Market value metrics saw some improvement month-over-month, largely driven by relative strength of Software and Services loans during the month, but remain suppressed overall.

US CLO secondary spreads widened in Q1. AAA’s tranche widened by 20bps, AA’s by 18, As by 29, BBBs by 77, BBs by 161 and Bs by 136. Meanwhile, the JP Morgan Leveraged Loan Index widened 87bps and the ICE BofA US HY Index widened 47bps.

Portfolio Strategy

The borrowing rate for leveraged loan companies remains high following rate increases from central banks in 2022 and 2023. However, borrowing rates moved lower following three rate cuts from the Fed at the end of 2025. The Fed has since shifted to a wait-and-see approach and held rates steady at their January meeting given solid economic growth, a stabilizing labor market and inflation above target. Markets now see the Fed on hold this year after partially pricing in a rate hike at the beginning of the month. This is a dramatic shift from year-end where more than 2 cuts were priced in.

Valuations have improved alongside the recent increase in volatility but still appear expensive overall. Given increasing tail risks in portfolios, we continue to prefer tranche purchases higher in the capital stack. However, selective shorter spread-duration assets for lower rated credits are starting to present more attractive entry points amid increasing geopolitical tensions and signs of a K-shaped economy, where middle and low economic earners continue to demonstrate weakness. This dynamic increases tail risks in portfolios, as evidenced by the recent selloff in the software sector, and underscores the need for rigorous fundamental credit analysis in CLOs, including stressing recoveries for distressed loans. Despite feeling that IG spreads are tight, we are finding value in AAA, AA, and A rated securities. We have also seen increased dispersion between managers lower in the capital stack, which could present attractive opportunities for select purchases of lower rated paper. We also expect there to be additional bouts of volatility in the coming months and would like to maintain the ability to shift further into lower rated tranches during future periods of market weakness. We previously preferred buying in the primary market, but following the software related selloff, buying in the secondary market has become more attractive.

Outlook

For risk assets generally, oil prices remain the key driver as investors grapple with a broadening supply shock, as well as hopes for deescalation of the Iran conflict. In the near term, we expect geopolitical uncertainty to remain elevated, contributing to increased market volatility and dispersion across issuers. This environment continues to reinforce a highly bifurcated loan market, where performing credits trade at relatively tight levels while a smaller group of stressed and idiosyncratic issuers drive downside risk and increase tail risk in portfolios.

While AI fears have taken a backseat following the onset of the war in Iran, the outcome is likely to have a bigger impact on long-term performance for loan issuers. We continue to see a repricing of risk premia in credit as a result of AI disintermediation in Software and other sectors as well as continued pressure in the Chemicals sector. We have seen this play out more acutely in the private credit market given the asset class’s high exposure to Software. However, while risks in the private credit market are notable, we don’t believe they pose a systemic threat and don't expect a broader market contagion.

Combined with geopolitical risks and risks to higher inflation, lower growth, and higher long-term rates, we believe that spreads are more likely to widen in the short-term and stay wider over the medium term. Given conservative portfolio positioning at the beginning of the year, we have been and continue to be well positioned to opportunistically add both in the primary and secondary markets across the cap stack, but particularly in strategies that can add BBB and lower rated tranches. However, the bifurcation in the market is notable, particularly for lower mezzanine and equity tranches. More stressed/seasoned CLOs (lower BB MVOCs and equity NAVs) are being heavily punished versus cleaner portfolios. As such, security selection is of paramount importance, and we remain highly selective when purchasing securities lower in the cap stack. We believe our bottoms-up approach of re-underwriting portfolios puts us in a good position to take advantage of security selection as spreads widen, focusing on credit selection.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. data.title: Procter & Gamble: Management Said The Worst Is Over, But Q3 Earnings May Be Key
data.description:
Introduction

The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) has had a rough year, down over 14% since February. After mixed Q2 results, the stock dipped, rallied, then dipped again.

Since February, the share price has been on a consistent decline. Impacted by tariffs and geopolitical tensions, I think the price decline in the past two months has more to do with higher inflation worries from the ongoing war with Iran.

While I do expect headwinds to persist, Q2 showed the worst is likely over and that PG could see sequential improvements going forward.

In this article, I discuss their performance, what investors should expect and look for in their upcoming earnings, and despite lingering risks, Procter & Gamble is a buy at current levels.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Procter & Gamble back in December in an article titled: Appears Undervalued - A Good Time To Nibble On This Dividend Legend.

At a forward P/E of 20.1x, PG traded below its 5-year average of 24.29x. Their A-rated balance sheet, well-covered dividend yield above 3%, and upside potential to their price target of $160 made them a buy for income investors.

Despite this, I did warn investors that continued softness in their segments could lead to lower earnings and volatility in the near term. But long-term investors should take advantage by dollar-cost averaging on any dips.

Since then, the share price has been volatile but relatively stable, down a modest 0.36%. This is in comparison to the S&P 500 (SP500), up 2.38% the past 4 months.

Performance So Far

As previously mentioned, PG is down double-digits in the past year. Since February, PG is down 14%. They reported Q2 earnings this past January and saw slight improvements from the prior quarter.

During my thesis in December, after Q1, PG managed a double beat on their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $1.99, while revenue was $22.39 billion. This was a beat of $0.09 and $220 million, respectively.

PG saw positive sales growth across all segments, with Beauty leading the way, up 6%. Grooming & Healthcare were up 5% and 2%, respectively, while Fabric & Baby, Feminine, and Family Care were all up 1%.

But Q2 saw EPS and revenue decline from the prior quarter. EPS was $1.88, while revenue was $22.21 billion. Results were mixed as EPS beat, while revenue missed.

The disappointing performance was due to declining margins. Both core and operating margins were down by 50 and 70 basis points. Procter & Gamble saw negative net earnings across all segments except for healthcare, up 2%.

Still, management reaffirmed its 2026 guidance. Organic sales growth is projected to be between 0% and 4%, while core EPS growth is expected to be the same.

Diluted EPS growth is expected to be between 1% and 6%. At the end of Q4'24 2024, initial guidance slated diluted EPS growth in a range of 3% to 9%. Net sales are expected to see slight growth between 1% and 5%.

This was different from the prior fiscal year, where they saw positive sales and EPS growth of 2% and 4%, respectively. While the expected growth is disappointing, the good news is that management said the worst is over.

Their CFO stated Q2 was their softest quarter. And the 3% dividend raise they conducted earlier this month could be an indication that headwinds are expected to dissipate. In my prior article, I mentioned that investors should expect a $0.03 to $0.05 increase going forward.

While headwinds persist, PG's dividend was well covered by cash flows with a 63.5% payout ratio through the first six months. With the raise, the quarterly dividend increased by approximately $0.03 from $1.0568 to $1.0885 a share.

Q3 Expectations Indicate The Worst May Not Be Over

Previously, management stated they expected improvements in the back half of the year due to investments, pricing, and product innovation.

But according to analysts, PG is expecting more headwinds. Since then, the company has seen 15 downward revisions and 0 upward. Both EPS and revenue are expected to decline from the previous two quarters.

While management stated Q2 was their softest quarter, Q3 could cause more volatility and will likely be a confirmation moment. If they do see soft earnings, their worst-quarter scenario gets pushed out. And I expect them to potentially reach a new 52-week low.

But I'm confident PG could see some stabilization in Q3. I'm expecting a beat on both, with EPS between $1.58 and $1.65 and revenue around $21 billion. Investors should look for improvements in the company's segments.

Q3 will be key. If segments continue to see challenges, this could force management to revise guidance. And lead to more price volatility and underperformance for the near to medium term.

Balance Sheet & Buybacks

A positive is that their A-rated balance sheet gives them the flexibility to continue supporting the dividend during tough times. In Q2, liquidity was robust with close to $11 billion available and $25.6 billion in long-term debt.

Roughly $11 billion of debt is due within the year, but their liquidity position was strong. PG has also been buying back shares. For the fiscal year, they expect to buy back $5 billion and return $15 billion to shareholders.

Shares outstanding declined slightly from 2.458 billion to 2.424 billion. Buybacks do show that the company is in a healthy position.

Attractive For Income, Not So Much For Growth

If you're an income investor, then PG's 3% forward yield and payout ratio are incentives to invest. But I wouldn't expect much upside, at least for another 6 to 12 months.

Procter & Gamble's forward P/E of 20.6x appears attractive, especially compared to its 5-year average of 24.14x. And although they offer solid upside right now, I believe this will hinge on the upcoming quarter.

If they manage to see vast improvements in their segments, then I see the stock potentially hitting $150-$155 by year's end. But if earnings decline and margins contract further, then investors should expect the stock to fall further and possibly hit a new low.

Risks & Takeaway

The Middle East conflict remains a huge risk for Procter & Gamble. If a peace deal does take 6 months to finalize, this will likely fuel inflation as oil and gas prices remain high.

Last month saw inflation rise 0.2% from the prior month. And the longer a deal takes, oil could surge back above $100 and stay there.

If so, I suspect this could throw a wrench in PG's turnaround and result in a weaker consumer, causing them to look to cheaper, off-brands. Ultimately, impacting PG's sales & volumes.

This means margins could contract further as sales & volumes remain muted or even negative. Going forward, Procter & Gamble is likely to see underperformance unless they see vast improvements within their segments in the upcoming quarter.

While I do maintain my buy rating for income investors and think most headwinds are priced in here, investors should keep a close eye on Q3 earnings. This will likely be a key indicator of how the price will perform for the remainder of the year.

data.title: Sodexo: The Results Proved Me Wrong (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Sodexo (SDXOF) (SDXAY) was a new and relatively small addition to my otherwise concentrated value portfolio this year. Whenever I buy a company, I plan to hold it for years and even decades, rather than weeks or months.

It's rare that I sell a stock after just a short time of holding it, but in the case of Sodexo, I did just that. I bought my shares at around €42 and sold them for €41 today. That's the power of having a margin of safety. In many cases, even if we're wrong, the mistakes aren't that costly.

Here's another important concept: when the facts change, I do change my mind. And in this case, the facts did change after the recently released half-year fiscal 2026 report. In my opinion, the report doesn't show a short-term blip that I could simply ignore, but more like systemic issues that have finally become impossible to hide. That's a huge difference.

I don't sell companies because of a bad quarter or two, but in the case of Sodexo, the problems may run deeper.

It took me some time to digest all the information, but in the end, I decided that I didn't buy into a predictable low-growth company with a juicy and safe dividend yield, but rather into a turnaround play that could take years or, in the worst case, may not happen at all. Too much risk for me, hence the downgrade to "Sell".

If Sodexo were to return to its old profitability and growth rates over the next few years, the upside could be substantial. But it's no longer the safe dividend play that I perceived it to be, and personally, I can find better opportunities out there with a clearer path to decent total returns.

So, where did I get Sodexo wrong? In my initial analysis a little over a month ago, I highlighted that the company's FCF generation had been reliable over decades. I attributed this to the long-running contracts and the high retention rates that should make cash flows quite predictable going forward. I believe this assessment is still right, but I didn't factor in that Sodexo has been underinvesting for years, and higher CapEx going forward could lead to substantially lower profits.

In general, I'm not a big fan of low-margin businesses, but in the case of Sodexo, I turned a blind eye to the 3% net margin due to those long-running and mostly inflation-protected contracts. I also ignored the first guidance cut last year, although I should have known that there might be more cockroaches in the kitchen. It was a mistake, and the H1 results quickly reminded me of my misjudgment.

A decline in profitability was expected this fiscal year, which the management labeled as a transition year. But the magnitude of the decline and the once again lowered guidance showcase that problems run deeper than I anticipated.

It's a powerful reminder that a slight decline in revenue or a small rise in costs can lead to dramatic declines in profits for low-margin businesses. As a long-term investor, unless we're dealing with a very high-quality business, it's best to stay away from low-margin businesses.

Let's quickly go through the half-year 2026 numbers to see what went wrong and why I believe the risk/reward profile has become more unfavorable despite the share price drop since my initial analysis.

Sodexo's H1 2026 Was Bad

It's not uncommon to see heightened volatility on an earnings release day. In some cases, a big drop is unwarranted, and I view it as a good opportunity to buy more shares of companies that I understand at a discount, but not this time.

On release day, Sodexo's shares initially declined by more than 16%, and my desire to buy more shares was nonexistent. The market was shocked by the report, and so was I.

Revenue came in at €12.01 billion, down 3.7%, but up by 1.6% on a constant currency basis. The underlying operating profit margin fell from 5.2% to 3.7%.

Operating profit fell by 46.2%. Net profit is down by 56.7%, and adjusted net profit fell from €450 million to €285 million, or by 36.7%. Ouch.

Slightly declining revenue had to be expected because the Euro appreciated against most currencies over the last year, and most of Sodexo's business is outside Europe. It's the dramatic margin decline that was unexpected.

Before the earnings release, management guided for slightly lower operating margins, but a decline from 5.2% to 3.7% is not "slight". And it gets worse: for the full year, the company expects an underlying operating profit margin between 3.2% and 3.4%.

Organic revenue guidance has also been cut once again, from a range of +1.5% to 2.5% down to +0.5% to +1%. Lower revenue guidance and a lower operating profit margin result in rapidly declining net profits.

Sodexo is Investing in a Turnaround

Last year, the underlying operating profit margin came in at 4.7%, so a decline to 3.2% and 3.4% is worrisome. To be fair, most of the margin decline can be attributed to investments and a review of contracts and assets.

It's fair to assume if those investments and reviews weren't made, that the decline in operations, which was a 0.50% margin decline in the half-year, would accelerate from here.

It shows that Sodexo's underlying challenges are greater than I anticipated. We're not dealing with a company that simply had a weak year or two; at this point, it's turning into a turnaround situation where the duration and the outcome are uncertain. It was Warren Buffett who said that "turnarounds seldom turn".

Labeling Sodexo a turnaround play when most of the margin decline came from investments and reviews might sound quite pessimistic. And here I have to quote Buffett again: "There is seldom just one cockroach in the kitchen". In my opinion, at this point, it's prudent to expect more bad news.

The CEO admitted that the causes are deep-rooted and long-standing. It's likely they underinvested for years, and higher investments could hurt profitability for quite some time.

I'm not pessimistic when it comes to Sodexo over the very long term. After all, it's a family-controlled business with a long history in a historically stable industry. The Bellon family has a great interest in ensuring that this business remains a source of income over the next decades, as a 50-year agreement prevents them from selling their shares. But it's fairly safe to say that over the next few years, heightened investments and debt repayments could hurt shareholder returns. I'm not waiting that long when better opportunities are all around me.

Declining Profitability Leads to Higher Debt Levels

Because of the declining profitability, the debt levels are becoming a greater burden. At the end of H1 2026, net debt stood at €3.6 billion for a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.7x. The company now expects to end the year above its target range of 1x to 2x net debt/EBITDA.

In my opinion, Sodexo can be considered a low-growth company. The main attraction was the dividend, which was well above 6% when I bought the shares. As a registered long-term shareholder, you can get 10% on top after holding the shares for four years. And since I tend to hold my shares for decades rather than month, it would have resulted in a 7% starting yield over the long term. Not bad.

I considered the dividend to be somewhat safe, but the half-year report changed my view.

Chances of Dividend Cut Are Rising

Given the declining profitability and the debt levels, I believe the chances of a substantial dividend cut are rising. During the conference call, management was asked what will happen to the distributions, to which they replied that it was too early to tell. Not a good sign in my view.

Sodexo paid €393 million in dividends, or €2.70 a share, last year. According to my calculations, EPS could come in at €3.17 this year. Given the elevated debt levels (compared to the depressed profits), the dividend seems endangered.

€765 million in operating profit - €140 million in interest expenses - 26% tax rate = €462.5 million net profit or an EPS of €3.17

I don't care about a year of lower profits and dividend payments as long as the long-term earnings power of a company stays intact. So, the main question remains how deep and how long the decline will be. It's a question to which I don't have a clear answer at the moment, hence the sellout.

The company expects to provide more information in July, and I will join the event, not as a shareholder but as an observer. It will certainly be interesting to watch the company from the sidelines.

Conclusion

In my twenty years of investing, I've made plenty of mistakes. Sodexo is one of them. The timing of my initial analysis couldn't have been worse, and it's a perfect reminder that low margins don't matter until they do. At the time of the purchase, the shares were already trading at single-digit P/E ratios with a large margin of safety. I am grateful that the market allowed me to offload my shares at only a modest loss.

With the half-year results, the risk profile of Sodexo has changed dramatically in my view. It is now a turnaround situation with an uncertain outcome. The dividend that I perceived to be quite safe isn't as certain as I'd like it to be.

While I believe the company has the potential to do quite well over the long term, I'm not willing to wait for a turnaround, that, in the worst case, won't ever happen. For the remaining Sodexo shareholders, I wish you the best, and I will keep covering the company going forward.
