data.title: REITs Are Still Misunderstood By Most Investors
data.description:
I have invested in REITs for well over a decade and write weekly articles covering them here on Seeking Alpha.

As a result, I am regularly exchanging thoughts with other investors about REITs, and this has made it very clear to me that, to this day, the vast majority of investors still don't understand the sector properly.

In today's article, I am going to discuss the 6 biggest misconceptions about REITs that I constantly come across on this platform:

Misconception #1: Commercial Real Estate Is Imploding

The media is full of stories about how "commercial real estate" is crashing down. In reality, what is typically happening is that reporters are using "commercial real estate" as a synonym for office real estate, when in reality, they shouldn't, as commercial real estate ranges across over 20 different property sectors.

So yes, the office sector is struggling, and in many cases, values are dropping. However, the office segment represents only 3% of the REIT sector.

The vast majority of REITs invest in much stronger property sectors that continue to perform well. This includes things like e-commerce warehouses, data centers, cell towers, senior housing, or even billboards.

So no, commercial real estate is not imploding. Valuations are stable for the most part.

Misconception #2: REITs Are Low-Return, Income Investments for Retirees

REITs have performed poorly over the last 5 years as they have faced a series of unfortunate headwinds.

First, the pandemic was a big headwind for REITs as we essentially shut down the outside world, hurting many property sectors. Then came the historic surge in interest rates, which drove capital away from REITs into fixed income. Finally, REITs faced a large wave of new supply deliveries, which were the result of near-0% interest rates that followed the pandemic.

All of this led to slow cash flow growth and compressed valuation multiples:

As a result, many investors now think that REITs are low-return investments that only make sense for retirees who need safe income.

But in reality, such periods of poor performance happen across every sector, as various assets go in and out of favor.

Over the longest time period that exists, REITs have actually beaten even the S&P 500 (SPY) and tech stocks (QQQ):

Some investors try to discredit this outperformance by claiming that it is the result of declining interest rates, but this alone cannot be the case, as all asset classes benefit from lower interest rates. In fact, it could be argued that tech stocks benefited a lot more than REITs, as they earn longer-duration cash flows, and as a result of this, also enjoyed greater multiple expansion over time.

Simple math also disproves this claim. If you buy a property at an 8% cap rate, enjoy 2% average annual growth, add some leverage at a 5% interest rate, and then deduct 50 basis points annually for management, you end up with 12-15% annual returns.

Real estate is simply a very lucrative asset class, and REITs make it even more rewarding as they enjoy large economies of scale, better access to financing, and other benefits.

Misconception #3: REITs Don't Enjoy the Benefits of Leverage

Lots of real estate investors imagine that REITs don't enjoy the benefits of leverage. They point out that they can use a mortgage when buying private property but not when investing in REITs.

In reality, this is nothing more than a misunderstanding of how the stock market works.

What's traded on the stock market is the equity value, not the total asset value. It is the equivalent of your down payments when buying a property. REITs then take this equity and add debt on top of it to earn higher returns for their shareholders.

Nearly all REITs use leverage, and so investors enjoy its benefits. The difference is simply that what you see fluctuate on the stock market is your equity value, not the total asset value.

REIT leverage is even better as it comes without loan origination fees, no liability risk, and often better terms than you could obtain yourself.

Misconception #4: REIT Dividend Yields Are Too Low

Investors will often also look at the dividend yields of major REIT ETFs like the Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) and conclude that their 3-4% dividend yields are too low.

But what these investors ignore is that these big ETFs are market-cap weighted and will typically allocate the bulk of their assets into mega-cap REITs, which happen to focus on high-growth (but low-yield) sectors like data centers, cell towers, and e-commerce warehouses. Moreover, those big REITs will typically use little leverage and retain a large portion of their cash flow to reinvest in growth.

It then isn't surprising that these ETFs only offer low yields.

But that does not mean that all REITs are low-yielding investments. Our highest-yielding REIT investment is NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP), which is offering a 12% dividend yield. Its dividend has grown consistently over the years and should be sustainable:

There are lots of good REITs offering 6-8% yields if you know where to look. Even some blue-chip REITs like Realty Income (O) will occasionally sell at a near 6% yield.

Misconception #5: Rising Interest Rates Are A Huge Issue for REITs

Investors will often also avoid REITs because they think that rising interest rates are a dealbreaker.

In reality, REITs use little leverage these days. The average LTV is only about 35%, and therefore, the impact of higher interest rates is not that significant. In fact, it may even benefit them over time by reducing the growth of new supply and resulting in faster rent growth.

This explains why REIT cash flows kept rising in 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025, despite the surge in interest rates. The growth slowed as interest expense increased, but this is not as big a deal as investors seem to imagine.

And again here, simple math can disprove this theory. As we noted earlier, if you buy a property at an 8% cap rate, enjoy 2% average annual growth, add 50% leverage at a 5% interest rate, and then deduct 50 basis points annually for management, you end up with 14% annual returns.

Many REITs have managed to access debt at a near 5% interest rate in the past year, but even if you increase that to say 6%, your returns would still remain solid and possibly even unchanged if it then leads to faster rent growth.

So no, the higher interest rates are not a dealbreaker for REITs.

It would be different if they were heavily leveraged and had lots of low-interest-rate debt to refinance in the years ahead, but this is simply not the case.

Misconception #6: REITs Are Just Value-Traps

Finally, after years of underperformance, many investors are now convinced that REITs won't ever make a comeback. They are value-traps and should be avoided.

But history shows that recent underperformers are often the future's outperformers, as valuations drop to low levels, and eventually the narrative changes and capital flows back.

Historically, whenever REITs have traded at steep discounts to their net asset values, they have been very rewarding in the following years:

And today, their valuations are some of the lowest ever recorded:

When valuations are so low, you don't need heroic assumptions to earn good returns. That is why I am particularly comfortable allocating heavily in REITs today.

Their time will come again sometime in the future, and I think that it could be already in the near-term as more investors become more concerned about AI disruption and decide to invest more heavily in AI-proof asset classes like REITs.

I think that this could push REITs back to new all-time highs, resulting in significant upside, especially if you buy the right types of REITs today. We have positioned our portfolio accordingly at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Stewardship Across Generations: A Stronger Model For Japan's Boards
data.description:
By Bob Herr, Zhiyuan Tao, CFA and Haruna Usui, CMA

Japanese companies favor seniority, but there may be material benefits to multigenerational boards.

Japan has made major strides in corporate governance over the past decade. Reforms have included increasing board independence and modernizing committee structures. Yet one component of Japanese boards remains relatively unchanged: age. That’s a material oversight, in our view. Corporate boards that are too monolithic could be putting a damper on profits.

Corporate boards in Japan have long been characterized by seniority and continuity against a backdrop of lifetime employment. More than 95% of directors in the TOPIX 100 are in the bubble generation or older, while fewer than 1% are under the age of 50. This level of experience provides stability and institutional knowledge, but it may also entrench decision-making and hinder capital efficiency.

Many boards in Japan prioritize balance sheet safety over returning capital to shareholders and taking calculated risks. This has helped contribute to a more than 10% gap in return on equity (ROE) between Japanese and US equities. In a market long challenged by poor capital allocation, we think multigenerational boards can help buck this trend.

Multigenerational Boards Can Boost Performance

Studies in both the US and Europe present a clear link between multigenerational boards and financial performance. He, Miletkov and Staneva found that companies with younger directors not only generate higher return on assets but also command higher price-to-book values—particularly for firms that invest more in R&D and engage in patenting activity.

Younger boards can also mean less exposure to defaults—and chicanery. Janahi, Millo and Voulgaris discovered that banks with multigenerational boards experience fewer nonperforming loans, while Neukirchen, Posch and Betzer observed less corporate misconduct among firms with a greater age range.

These findings cumulatively suggest that multigenerational boards have the potential to improve capital allocation, reduce risk and boost valuations.

Mind the Gaps: Age and ROE

Our own in-house research confirms these findings. We tracked TOPIX constituents over a 10-year period—the largest study of its kind. The results were striking.

Firms with a more than 30-year age gap between the youngest and oldest director—what we define as multigenerational boards—delivered ROE more than 200 basis points higher, on average, than companies with more senior boards. This outperformance occurred in every calendar year during the period, and the results were statistically significant across sectors. Multigenerational boards achieved superior ROE in all but one sector, with the dispersion independent of size, style or founder-led status (Display).

Why the improved performance under multigenerational boards? We theorize that younger directors counterbalance the risk-averse tendencies of more senior directors. Prior academic research shows that younger boards exhibit greater risk tolerance, on average, than their senior counterparts. This is reflected in increased M&A activity and lower cash balances.

Of course, experience and qualifications remain critically important to board appointments. We favor a balanced approach that preserves legacy institutional knowledge while opening the door to next-generation dynamism.

What does this look like in practice?

Hello Kitty’s Generational Glow-Up

Sanrio (SNROF), the company behind the popular Hello Kitty brand, has long held a rich portfolio of valuable intellectual property. But historically, its business model focused largely on domestic merchandise sales. The company maintained consistent leadership for many decades under its founder, Shintaro Tsuji, which contributed to stability but slowed strategy development. As market conditions evolved and retail trends shifted, Sanrio’s merchandise business came under pressure, and the company recorded a loss in fiscal year 2020.

Recognizing the need to adapt, the 92-year-old Tsuji turned leadership of the company over to his 31-year-old grandson but remained on the board. Sanrio’s young new president initiated sweeping reforms by refreshing the board and management, overhauling retail operations, and recruiting external talent with diverse backgrounds.

The company also expanded its international footprint and leveraged social media and streaming services to amplify its brand. These changes unlocked the global potential of Sanrio’s character portfolio and repositioned the firm as an entertainment-focused intellectual-property enterprise, rather than a traditional retailer.

The effect on operating profit and ROE has been dramatic (Display). Sanrio now expects operating profit of ¥75.1 billion in FY 2025—more than triple its previous peak of ¥21 billion in FY 2013. Moreover, the company’s stock price has increased tenfold, reflecting renewed investor confidence in its strategy and earnings potential.

Sanrio’s transformation illustrates how a fresh generational perspective can address underlying issues hindering shareholder returns. In our view, if properly implemented, multigenerational boards can unlock organizational agility, greater independence and new pathways for value creation. In an era of rapid market and technological change, we believe boards that balance experience with fresh perspectives can be catalysts for building shareholder value.

data.title: Macro Insights: The 'Spoils Of The Iran War' Hidden In The Market
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from April’s first half…

Trending Themes

The Iran Conflict and the Hormuz Siege

Sell The Rally - There Is No Easy Way Out Of Iran War | Damir Tokic | 04/01/26

“The stock market bounced, driven by Iran war headlines, but the bounce is likely to be short-lived, as the macro situation remains vulnerable. The endgame for the Iran war is still uncertain, and oil prices are likely to remain high even if the war “freezes,” which seems to be the current situation. Negotiations are likely to fail, while the US seems unable to bear the economic and political costs of a ground invasion.”

3 Reasons Why There Will Be No Ceasefire In Iran | Robert & Sam Kovacs | 04/06/26

“Markets are clinging to hope of a ceasefire, which seems structurally impossible. The markets remain complacent about energy and its outlook; pricing reflects hope rather than market structure. Here are three reasons why there will be no deal by tomorrow.”

Why The U.S. Blockade May Be The Move That Ends The War | Deep Value Investing | 04/13/26

“It is my view that the U.S. blockade of Iran-bound commercial ships is mainly about regaining leverage for dismantling Iran’s nuclear program and reopening the Strait of Hormuz.”

Iran: A Game Theory Analysis Of The Hormuz Siege | Brendan O'Boyle | 04/15/26

“Markets are underestimating the risk of a prolonged Strait of Hormuz (SoH) closure, which threatens 11% of global oil supply and could trigger recession. Current equity valuations reflect optimism for a quick SoH resolution, but game theory and history suggest a drawn-out, attritional conflict is likely.”

Market Dynamics

A Major Market Rotation Is Likely Coming - Here's Where I'm Loading Up | Samuel Smith | 04/02/26

“The first quarter of 2026 was one of the most headline-whiplash quarters investors have experienced in years, with numerous forces driving market rotations…”

Why It May Be Time To Load Up Soon | Victor Dergunov | 04/03/26

“Current market volatility is driven by geopolitical risks, technical breakdowns, and sentiment, decoupling equities from fundamentals…A risk-on environment is expected to return post-Iran conflict, potentially driving 50-100% appreciation in select high-quality equities over 12-18 months.”

This Is A Most Compelling Buying Opportunity | Bill Gunderson | 04/06/26

“I believe the market is forming a major bottom, presenting a compelling buying opportunity despite recent volatility. Earnings growth remains robust, with S&P 500 Q1 2026 estimates at 13.2% and revenue growth at 9.7%, defying bearish sentiment. The forward P/E for the S&P 500 has compressed to 19.8, below its five-year average, while earnings estimates are at record highs.”

This Rally Won't End Well | James Foord | 04/15/26

“The S&P 500's resilience near 7,000 is driven by hedged positioning, CTA flows, liquidity tailwinds, and continued AI trade momentum. Despite supportive flows, I expect significant downside in the next two to three months due to geopolitical risks, persistent inflation, and adverse seasonality.”

Post-Conflict Plays

Finding The Opportunities After The Selloff And End Of The War | D.M. Martins Research | 04/03/26

“After a few false starts, enough signs have emerged recently that, certainly without any guarantees, we may be approaching the end of the most recent conflict in the Middle East-at least the uglier part of it… It begs the question: if or when the Iran war overhang subsides, where might the investment opportunities lie?”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

To stay current on the latest economic and market events coming up, subscribe to Wall Street Breakfast, Seeking Alpha's flagship daily business news summary.
